skill.name: openapi-generator
skill.description: Generate, improve, validate, and document OpenAPI or Swagger specifications for existing APIs.
skill.summary: Create practical OpenAPI specifications and API documentation from code, routes, and examples.
skill.author: CuriousLearner

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk matches, but the weak-cryptography findings are false positives from OpenAPI documentation terms and example auth fields. The skill contains no prompt injection evidence, but it does recommend package installation and external code-generation commands, so publication should include a warning about reviewing generated files and installed tools.

medium_findings.title: External Package and Code Generation Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill documents npm global installs, npx execution, CLI linting, conversion commands, and OpenAPI Generator output paths. These are legitimate API documentation workflows, but they can install third-party packages and write generated files into a project.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are visible in fenced documentation blocks and match the skill purpose. The risk is operational, not malicious, because no hidden execution or exfiltration behavior was found.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The detected URLs are example API servers, license links, OAuth endpoint placeholders, and public tool references. I did not find evidence that the skill sends data to these URLs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are embedded in OpenAPI sample content and tool listings. No code path or instruction transmits local files, credentials, or user data to them.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The high static findings align with ordinary OpenAPI terms such as API key, bearer token, OAuth, password field, HTTPS, and authentication documentation. I did not find weak hash algorithms, encryption routines, or credential handling code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited contexts are schema and documentation examples, not cryptographic implementations. The scanner likely matched security vocabulary rather than vulnerable code.

low_findings.title: System and Network Reconnaissance Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported reconnaissance locations describe API routes, fields, schema metadata, and generator targets. No evidence found of host discovery, network scanning, environment probing, or inventory collection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The static locations are inside API documentation examples. I did not find commands such as port scans, host enumeration, or network probing instructions.

dangerous_patterns.title: Third-Party CLI Installation and Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill suggests installing and running external CLI tools. Users should pin trusted versions, review package provenance, and inspect generated code before merging it.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is directly present in command examples. It is common for this skill type, but it changes the user's environment and can generate project files.

content.user_title: Generate OpenAPI Specs from Existing APIs
content.value_statement: Teams often have working APIs but incomplete or outdated OpenAPI documentation. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code draft, validate, and improve practical API specifications.
actual_capabilities: Draft OpenAPI 3.0 specifications from route descriptions, code examples, and API behavior.
Add paths, parameters, request bodies, responses, schemas, tags, and authentication schemes.
Improve existing Swagger or OpenAPI files with examples, descriptions, validation rules, and error responses.
Explain how to validate specifications with common tools such as Spectral.
Show command examples for generating clients, server stubs, documentation, and JSON conversions.
limitations: It cannot guarantee that generated specifications match runtime behavior without tests or live API verification.
It may require manual review for authentication, authorization scopes, and sensitive field exposure.
Generated SDKs and server stubs depend on third-party tools that should be reviewed before use.
It does not replace contract testing, security review, or API governance processes.

use_cases.title: Document an Existing Service
use_cases.description: Create a first OpenAPI draft from routes, controller code, and sample requests.

use_cases.title: Improve API Reference Quality
use_cases.description: Add examples, error responses, reusable schemas, and clearer descriptions to an existing spec.

use_cases.title: Prepare Client Generation
use_cases.description: Check a specification before generating SDKs or server stubs for downstream teams.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Spec
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a new API document.
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate an OpenAPI 3.0 specification for these endpoints. Include paths, methods, parameters, responses, and simple examples.

prompt_templates.title: Improve an Existing Spec
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this OpenAPI file and improve descriptions, examples, schema reuse, validation constraints, and error responses.

prompt_templates.title: Validate Contract Readiness
prompt_templates.scenario: Prepare for review or CI validation.
prompt_templates.prompt: Check this API specification for missing responses, inconsistent schemas, unclear auth requirements, and validation issues.

prompt_templates.title: Plan SDK Generation
prompt_templates.prompt: Prepare this OpenAPI spec for client SDK generation. Identify generator settings, naming risks, auth handling, and files to review after generation.

output_examples.input: A list of Express routes for users, sessions, and profile updates.
output_examples.output: A structured API specification plan with route groups, request parameters, response models, authentication notes, and example coverage.

output_examples.input: An existing OpenAPI draft with missing error responses.
output: A review summary of incomplete operations.
Suggested error response coverage for validation, authentication, authorization, and server failures.

output_examples.input: A finished OpenAPI file before client SDK generation.
output_examples.output: A readiness checklist covering schema consistency, operation names, authentication definitions, validation commands, and generated code review steps.

best_practices: Keep the specification in version control and update it with each API change.
Validate generated or edited specs with a linter before publishing documentation.
Review authentication, authorization, and sensitive fields with the API owner.
anti_patterns: Do not publish generated specifications without checking them against real API behavior.
Do not expose internal fields, secrets, or private error details in examples.
Do not run package installation or generator commands without reviewing the tool source and output path.

faq.question: Can this skill create a complete OpenAPI file from source code?
faq.answer: It can draft a strong specification from code and routes, but human review is still required.

faq.question: Does it support Swagger and OpenAPI?
faq.answer: Yes. The guidance focuses on OpenAPI 3.0 and also references Swagger ecosystem tools.

faq.question: Can it generate client SDKs?
faq.answer: It explains generator commands and preparation steps. Third-party generator output should be reviewed before use.

faq.question: Is it safe to run the included commands?
faq.answer: The commands are common examples, but they install or run external tools. Use trusted package versions and review outputs.

faq.question: Can it validate my API specification?
faq.answer: It can guide validation and identify likely issues. Use a real OpenAPI linter for final checks.

faq.question: Will it detect every security issue in an API contract?
faq.answer: No. It can document auth and sensitive fields, but it does not replace security testing or threat modeling.
